name: Motion Intent
summary: Protocol defining the application's physics, latency tolerance, and transition behavior.

physics.description: The animation model governing transitions and micro-interactions

description:
Physics Contract

  • natural: Mass-based physics (springs), context-aware transitions.
  • immediate: < 16ms latency. Disables decorative motion for high-performance workflows.
  • reduced: Semantic motion only (e.g. layout shifts), disabling parallax/scaling. Respects prefers-reduced-motion.

name: Density Intent
summary: Protocol defining spatial relationships, touch targets, and information quantity.

level.description: The spatial density profile controlling touch targets, spacing, and information quantity

description:
Spatial Contract

  • comfortable: 44px+ touch targets. Whitespace is used for grouping (Consumer standard).
  • compact: 32px targets. High information density, border-based grouping (Enterprise standard).
  • wafer: 24px targets. Maximum data visibility for complex tooling/IDE interfaces.

name: Interaction Intent
summary: Protocol defining the input modality (pointer, touch, spatial) that shapes interaction patterns.

modality.description: The primary input method determining interaction patterns

description:
Input Contract

  • pointer: Fine-grained control. Hover menus enabled, scrollbars visible.
  • touch: Coarse control. Swipe actions replace hover menus. Hit testing padding applied.
  • gamepad: Spatial Navigation (D-Pad). Focus is trapped within active regions (Sunsetting tab-index logic).

This intent owns the input modality only. The composite-widget focus contract — roving tabindex vs. virtual focus, arrows-within / Tab-between, and selection-follows-focus — is owned by the Focus Delegation Intent.

name: Focus Delegation Intent
summary: Protocol for composite-widget focus — roving tabindex vs. virtual focus (active descendant), arrow-key navigation within a widget, and whether selection follows focus.

strategy.description: How the active item is tracked as focus moves through the widget

orientation.description: The axis the arrow keys traverse

selectionFollowsFocus.description: Whether moving focus to an item also activates or selects it

description:
Tab vs. Arrows

A composite widget exposes one tab stop: Tab / Shift+Tab move between widgets, while the arrow keys move within — options, rows, and cells are reached by arrows, never by Tab.

Strategy

How the active item is tracked as focus moves.

  • roving: Real DOM focus moves between items — the active item has tabindex="0", all others -1. Use when the items themselves are the interactive targets (menus, toolbars, radio groups, tabs).
  • virtual: DOM focus stays on a controller element (a text input or the listbox) while the visually active item is tracked via aria-activedescendant. Required when typing must continue uninterrupted (comboboxes). The active item must be scrolled into view on each move — browsers do not auto-scroll aria-activedescendant targets the way they do real focus.
  • native: Browser-managed focus with no JavaScript — a radio group or a native <select>. The native-first baseline; reach for roving/virtual only when a custom widget rules native out.

Orientation

vertical binds Up/Down, horizontal binds Left/Right, and both enables two-dimensional movement (menubars, grids). Home/End jump to the first/last item regardless of orientation. Movement clamps at the ends by default; a widget may opt into wrapping (last → first).

Selection follows focus

  • follows: Moving focus immediately activates/selects the item — single-select listboxes and auto-activated tabs. Fast, but unsafe when activation is expensive or destructive.
  • independent: Focus moves without committing; selection happens on an explicit key (Space/Enter). Correct for multi-select, menus, and comboboxes.

Composition

This intent owns only focus movement. It composes with the Selection Intent (what gets chosen and when it commits), the Type-Ahead Intent (seeking an item by typing within the same focus model), the Anchor Intent (a menu or listbox surface adopts a focus strategy when it opens), and the Interaction Intent (input modality — pointer, touch, or spatial navigation).

Interface Protocol

type FocusStrategy = 'roving' | 'virtual' | 'native';
type Orientation = 'vertical' | 'horizontal' | 'both';

interface FocusDelegationIntent {
    // How the active item is tracked
    strategy: FocusStrategy;
    // Axis the arrow keys traverse
    orientation: Orientation;
    // Does moving focus also select?
    selectionFollowsFocus: boolean;
    // Wrap around at the ends, or clamp (default)
    wrap?: boolean;
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Defines the two focus-management techniques (roving tabindex and aria-activedescendant) and the arrows-within / Tab-between rule. We adopt both verbatim as the strategy axis and the core invariant.
Headless roving-focus primitive; its orientation and loop options map directly to our orientation dimension and wrap option, validating focus delegation as a standalone, composable concern.
Implements both strategies behind one hook and exposes selection-follows-focus via selectionBehavior. Confirms decoupling focus movement from selection commitment.
Single-tab-stop composite widgets with arrow navigation; tabs auto-activate (selection follows focus) while menus do not — informs the selectionFollowsFocus split.

uxResearch.title: Cross-cutting paradigms in the dropdown family
findings: Focus delegation is the keystone paradigm a dropdown composes — reused by listbox, menu, tree, grid, toolbar, and tabs.
Virtual focus requires manually scrolling the aria-activedescendant target into view; browsers do not do it automatically.
Select-only comboboxes keep DOM focus on the control and never let arrow navigation alone change the value (selection independent of focus).

researchGaps: Spatial / D-pad navigation overlaps the Interaction Intent's gamepad modality (focus trapping within active regions) — decide whether spatial navigation is a strategy here or stays owned by Interaction.
Reconcile selectionFollowsFocus with the Selection Intent's immediacy axis (live vs. buffered) — related but distinct commitment moments.
Two-dimensional grid mechanics (row/column wrap, cell vs. widget focus, ragged rows) need a deeper contract than the orientation:both value currently implies.

name: Live Region Status Intent
summary: Protocol for announcing transient status to assistive technology via a live region — urgency, the semantic container (status, alert, log), and what is announced (loading, result counts, no-results, errors). One shared region; sources announce by event and the announcer resolves a single winner by precedence (error > loading > count) with a short debounce.

urgency.description: How forcefully an update interrupts the user (maps to aria-live)

relevance.description: Whether the whole message or only new additions are announced

description:
Urgency

How forcefully an update interrupts, mapping to aria-live.

  • polite: Announced when the user is idle, without interrupting (advisory status — loading, result counts). The safe default.
  • assertive: Announced immediately, interrupting the current utterance. Reserve for time-critical messages (a submitted form's error); overuse is hostile.

Role

The semantic container. The region must already exist (empty) in the DOM when the page loads so assistive tech registers it; populate its text on events rather than inserting the node on demand.

  • status: role="status" — implicit aria-live="polite" + aria-atomic="true". Advisory status.
  • alert: role="alert" — implicit aria-live="assertive". Errors and urgent conditions.
  • log: role="log" — sequential additions where order matters (chat, console).

Relevance

  • atomic: The entire region is re-announced on change (aria-atomic="true") — for a single replaced message like "3 results available".
  • additions: Only newly added content is announced (aria-relevant="additions") — for append-only logs.

What to Announce

Surface state in text, never a spinner or colour alone:

  • Loading/searching: "Loading…" / "Searching…" (compose the Loader Intent for label conventions).
  • Result counts: "{n} results available", and "{label} {i} of {n}" as focus moves through options.
  • Empty: an explicit "No results found".
  • Errors: the validation message text (compose the Validation Intent).

Composition

This intent owns only the announcement channel. The content it announces is owned by neighbouring intents: Loader (pending text), Validation (errors), and Selection / combobox (counts). Do not move focus to the live region.

Precedence & coalescing

When several sources can write the one region, the announcer resolves a single winner rather than letting them clobber each other, and debounces so rapid updates don't flood assistive tech.

  • Precedence by kind — a fixed, extensible order decides the slot when updates land together: error > loading > count. Errors win, but stay polite and take the slot by precedence rather than escalating to a separate assertive region.
  • Debounce — coalesce bursts within a short settle window (~150 ms) so successive messages announce once, not per keystroke.

Through-the-DOM coordination

Sources never reference the announcer or an aria-live node directly — they coordinate through the DOM, so any number of independent surfaces compose with no wiring between them:

  • Input by event, not handle — a source dispatches new CustomEvent('statuschange', { bubbles: true, detail: { kind, message } }); the announcer listens on the host. No source holds a reference to the region.
  • One region, adopted by reference — resolve an existing status=<id> node first, else create one role="status" (implicit polite). Two sources must share one region — the no-double-announce invariant, proven by test.

Interface Protocol

type Urgency = 'polite' | 'assertive';
type LiveRole = 'status' | 'alert' | 'log';
type Relevance = 'atomic' | 'additions';

interface LiveRegionStatusIntent {
    // aria-live level
    urgency: Urgency;
    // Semantic container (sets the default urgency)
    role: LiveRole;
    // Whole message vs. only additions
    relevance?: Relevance;
}

type StatusKind = 'error' | 'loading' | 'count' | string;

interface StatusChangeDetail {
    kind: StatusKind;   // resolves precedence: error > loading > count
    message: string;    // the text announced
}

// Sources announce by event, never by reference:
//   host.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('statuschange', {
//     bubbles: true, detail: { kind, message },
//   }));
// The announcer owns the single region, precedence, and a ~150ms debounce.

Reference implementation

First implemented as the droplist announcer — frontierui:blocks/droplist/LiveStatus.ts — which owns the one shared region (stamping data-live-status to claim it), bridges filter-request → loading and filter-resolved → ready/empty, and exposes a generic statuschange event / announce() entry for any source. The one-region / no-double-announce invariant is proven in the droplist behavior tests.

Cross-cutting consumers — the same announce-transient-status pattern recurs across surfaces that should compose this intent instead of hand-rolling live regions: #013 focus announcements, #056 nav focus reset, #059 pagination announce, and #115 data-table announcements.

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: role=status implies polite + atomic; role=alert implies assertive. The region must pre-exist empty for AT to register it. We adopt the role→urgency default mapping as the contract.
The live region must exist on load; announce '{n} results available' politely. Assertive floods the user during rapid typing.
A centralized localized announcer for focus/filter/selection reuses one managed region across messages, rather than scattering aria-live nodes.

uxResearch.title: Announcing async combobox states
findings: No-results and result counts must be announced as text, not just shown visually.
Loading vs. empty need distinct, localizable strings.
Keep aria-expanded honest while a loading or empty popup is shown.

researchGaps: Reconcile with the Feedback and Message intents (toasts/alerts) which also convey severity — is live-region delivery a shared transport those compose, or a distinct channel?

name: Focus Containment Intent
summary: Protocol for overlay focus — confining keyboard focus to an active surface, making the rest of the page inert, where focus lands when it opens, and where it returns when it closes.

trap.description: Whether keyboard focus is confined to the surface while it is open

background.description: How the rest of the page behaves while the surface is open

initialFocus.description: Where focus lands when the surface opens

restore.description: Where focus returns when the surface closes

description:
Containment vs. delegation

This intent owns where focus is allowed to go while an overlay is open — trapping it inside the surface and sealing off the rest of the page. It is distinct from the Focus Delegation Intent, which owns how focus moves between items once it is inside a widget. A trapped dialog still delegates focus internally; the two compose.

Trap

  • trap: Tab / Shift+Tab cycle within the surface and never reach the page behind it. The accessibility requirement for any modal surface.
  • none: Focus may leave the surface by tabbing — correct for modeless popovers, non-modal drawers, and toolbars that sit in the page flow.

Background

How the rest of the document is treated while the surface is open. Prefer the native inert attribute on the background over manually juggling tabindex.

  • inert: The background is marked inert — unfocusable, unclickable, and hidden from assistive tech. The native mechanism behind a true modal. <dialog>.showModal() applies this automatically to everything outside the dialog.
  • interactive: The background stays live and operable — a modeless surface that contains focus only while focus happens to be inside it.

Initial focus

  • first-tabbable: Focus the first tabbable element inside the surface. The safe default for forms.
  • container: Focus the surface container itself (it takes tabindex="-1") — use when there is no obvious first control, or the first control would be a destructive button.
  • explicit: Focus a designated element (e.g. a Close button on a confirmation dialog, or the primary input).

Restore

  • restore: Return focus to the element that opened the surface when it closes. The default — losing the user's place is disorienting.
  • none: Do not move focus on close — when the trigger is gone or the next focus target is elsewhere by design.
  • explicit: Send focus to a designated element on close (e.g. a newly revealed region the surface was gating).

Composition

A surface adopts containment when it opens. The Modal Intent composes this for its focus management rather than redefining trap/restore inline; the Anchor Intent composes it for modal-mode popovers and menus; the Focus Delegation Intent governs movement within the contained region. Focus indication (:focus-visible styling) is a separate visual concern and is not owned here.

Interface Protocol

type Trap = 'trap' | 'none';
type Background = 'inert' | 'interactive';
type InitialFocus = 'first-tabbable' | 'container' | 'explicit';
type Restore = 'restore' | 'none' | 'explicit';

interface FocusContainmentIntent {
    // Confine focus to the surface?
    trap: Trap;
    // Seal off the rest of the page (native inert) vs. leave it live
    background: Background;
    // Where focus lands on open
    initialFocus: InitialFocus;
    // Where focus returns on close
    restore: Restore;
    // Selector, when initialFocus or restore is 'explicit'
    target?: string;
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Defines the modal-dialog contract: trap focus inside, render the rest inert, set initial focus on open, and restore focus to the trigger on close. We adopt all four as the dimensions of this intent.

showModal() natively traps focus and inert-s everything outside the dialog; inert is the platform mechanism for sealing the background. The native-first baseline — reach for a JS focus trap only when <dialog> is ruled out.

Headless focus-scope primitive whose trapped / mount-focus / unmount-focus props map directly to our trap, initialFocus, and restore dimensions — validating containment as a standalone, composable concern.
Exposes containment as contain + restoreFocus + autoFocus on one scope, separate from collection navigation — confirms decoupling containment from focus delegation.

researchGaps: Nested/stacked traps — when a dialog opens another, define how the inner trap nests over the outer and how restore unwinds the stack (relates to the Modal Intent's stackable/recede/advance lifecycle).

Focus-trap edge guards vs. native inert — when <dialog> is unavailable, decide whether the recommended fallback is sentinel tabbable guards or programmatic wrap, and how it interacts with browser/AT virtual cursors.

name: Windowed Collection Intent
summary: Protocol for rendering only the visible window of a large collection (virtualization) while preserving set-size and position semantics and keeping keyboard focus valid.

rendering.description: Whether all items are in the DOM or only a visible window

itemSize.description: Whether row sizes are known ahead of time

dataSource.description: Whether the full item count is known or fetched progressively

description:
Rendering

  • full: All items live in the DOM. Correct for short lists — simplest and most accessible.
  • windowed: Only the visible window (plus a small overscan) is rendered; rows recycle as the user scrolls. Reach for it only when the item count is large enough to hurt layout/memory — not by default.

Set-Size Semantics

Because a windowed list's DOM no longer reflects the true collection, every rendered item must carry aria-setsize (the full count) and aria-posinset (its absolute index + 1, not its DOM position) so assistive tech announces "item 42 of 5000". Never derive position from DOM order.

Focus Persistence

The currently focused or active item must not be unmounted when scrolled out of the window, or DOM focus and aria-activedescendant references break. This composes the Focus Delegation Intent: under virtual focus the active item must also be scrolled back into view on keyboard navigation.

Item Size

  • fixed: Uniform row height — scroll offsets are pure arithmetic; cheapest and most stable.
  • variable: Measured/estimated heights — requires a measurement cache and can cause scroll-anchor shifts.

Data Source

  • bounded: The full count is known; the window maps into a complete dataset.
  • infinite: Items are fetched progressively (infinite scroll / load-more). Composes the Loader Intent loadingMore state — render an announced progress row at the list end rather than a blocking overlay.

Composition

This intent owns only which items are realised in the DOM. Selection, focus movement, and async fetching are delegated to the Selection, Focus Delegation, and Loader intents respectively.

Interface Protocol

type Rendering = 'full' | 'windowed';
type ItemSize = 'fixed' | 'variable';
type DataSource = 'bounded' | 'infinite';

interface WindowedCollectionIntent {
    // All items vs. visible window
    rendering: Rendering;
    // Known vs. measured row heights
    itemSize?: ItemSize;
    // Known count vs. progressive fetch
    dataSource?: DataSource;
    // Extra rows rendered beyond the viewport
    overscan?: number;
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: setsize/posinset let assistive tech report position and total when not all items are in the DOM — the core accessibility contract for virtualization.
Persists the focused element in the DOM even when scrolled out of view and supplies positional ARIA so screen-reader context is not lost.
Headless windowing engines; fixed vs. variable sizing and overscan are the primary tuning axes, which we expose as dimensions rather than reimplement.

uxResearch.title: Virtualization accessibility caveats
findings: Set aria-setsize/aria-posinset on every rendered option using the absolute index, not DOM order.
Never unmount the focused/active option while it is scrolled out of the window.
Infinite scroll should expose a load-more progress row with an aria-label.

researchGaps: Threshold guidance — at what item count does windowing pay off versus its accessibility and scroll-anchor cost? Provide a heuristic rather than 'always virtualize'.
Find-in-page (Ctrl+F) and full-text search break when matching items are not realised in the DOM — define a fallback (render-on-search or a search index).

name: Prefetch Intent
summary: Controls the eagerness of resource prefetching based on connection and power.

eagerness.description: How proactively the application anticipates the user's next action

suppressWhen.description: Conditions that suppress prefetching to respect the user's environment

description:
Eagerness

How proactively the application anticipates the user's next action. Higher eagerness improves perceived speed but consumes more resources.

  • viewport: Prefetch when the link becomes visible to the user (Lazy).
  • hover: Prefetch when the user shows interest (hovering or focusing) (Moderate).
  • interaction: Prefetch at the moment the user begins to act (pointer down) (Eager).

User Conditions

Conditions under which prefetching should be suppressed to respect the user's environment. The implementing block decides which APIs to use for detection.

  • limited-data: User has indicated a preference to conserve data (e.g., metered connection).
  • low-power: Device is in a low-battery or power-saving state.
  • slow-connection: Network quality is too poor for speculative loading to be beneficial.

Interface Protocol

type PrefetchEagerness = 'viewport' | 'hover' | 'interaction';
type PrefetchConstraint = 'limited-data' | 'low-power' | 'slow-connection';

interface PrefetchIntent {
    // How proactively to prefetch
    eagerness: PrefetchEagerness;
    // Conditions that suppress prefetching
    suppressWhen: PrefetchConstraint[];
}

name: Loader Intent
summary: Protocol defining how a pending state affects the user interface via Blocking, Scoping, and Timing profiles. Covers anti-flicker thresholds, loader-vs-skeleton indicators, determinate progress, and long-wait escalation (warn / go-async), plus version token tracking and hierarchy aggregation for async lifecycle management.

strategy.description: How the UI is blocked during a pending state

scope.description: What part of the UI is affected by the pending state

progress.description: Whether the user can see measurable progress

context.description: Whether prior content exists when loading begins

indicator.description: What kind of indeterminate indicator is shown

holdFloor.description: Whether a shown indicator is held for a minimum duration to prevent flash

escalation.description: How a wait that exceeds expected duration is surfaced

description:
Blocking Strategy

How the UI responds to a pending state. Each strategy represents a different trade-off between user freedom and data integrity.

  • optimistic: Non-blocking. UI remains fully interactive (background sync). The predicted outcome is shown immediately; reverted on failure.
  • soft: Input-blocking. Applies aria-disabled and pointer-events: none. Content remains visible but non-interactive.
  • hard: Visual-blocking. Content is obscured or overlaid (Backdrop). User must wait.
  • replacement: Content-blocking. Placeholder content (Skeleton, Spinner) temporarily replaces the view.

Scope Definition

What part of the UI is affected by the pending state.

  • element: Constrained to the triggering target (e.g., Button spinner).
  • region: Constrained to the containing landmark (e.g., aria-busy on the Form).
  • viewport: Global interruption (Glass pane).

Progress Indication

Whether the user can see measurable progress or only that activity is happening.

  • determinate: Progress can be measured (0–100%). Rendered as a progress bar or percentage. Research suggests this is preferred for operations exceeding 10 seconds.
  • indeterminate: Progress is happening but completion is unknown. Rendered as a spinner, pulse, or shimmer animation. Most web waits are this case — the duration cannot be known up front.

Determinate guidance: when a real measure exists (file upload/download), show a true progress bar reflecting transfer only — it excludes server-side processing time, so don't leave the bar stuck at 100%; if processing follows, show an indeterminate loader after the bar completes. Consider starting the bar slightly above 0% so slow-to-start operations feel responsive. Run multiple transfers in parallel (not sequentially), make each individually retryable, and don't let one failure abort the whole batch unless the items are dependent.

Indicator

What kind of indeterminate indicator is shown. Both signal that work is happening; they differ in what they tell the user about the incoming content.

  • loader: An animation (e.g. spinner, pulse, shimmer). Signals activity but conveys nothing about the shape of the content to come.
  • skeleton: A placeholder shaped like the content that will (or may) load. Sets the user's expectation of the content's nature and limits layout shift when real content arrives; subtly animated to indicate ongoing work. Preferred for known layouts (the replacement strategy over initial context).

Loading Context

Whether prior content exists. This changes user expectations for how the pending state should look.

  • initial: No prior content is available. The user expects a placeholder (skeleton, spinner, empty state).
  • transition: Prior content is already visible. The user expects the existing content to remain visible (dimmed, stale) while new content loads. Replacing visible content with a loading indicator is perceived as regression.

Timing Contract

Maps semantic timing profiles to configurable durations (ms). Applications can override defaults or register new profiles.

  • immediate: Defaults to 0ms (Configurable).
  • debounced: Defaults to 400ms (Configurable). Prevents flicker for fast operations.
  • [custom]: User-defined keys (e.g., slow: 1000ms).

Anti-Flicker

A loader must never flash. Two paired guards form one anti-flicker contract — both are defaults and configurable, and the exact values should be confirmed against a test/tool:

  • Entry threshold (timing): only show the indicator once the wait passes a threshold (≈50ms), so quick operations show nothing at all.
  • Minimum hold (holdFloor): once shown, keep the indicator visible for a floor duration (≈100ms) even if the operation resolves sooner, so it never appears-and-vanishes in a flash. holdFloor: minimum is the default; none hides the instant the operation resolves.

Long Waits & Going Async

How a wait that runs longer than expected is surfaced (escalation). This is about a still-succeeding operation taking too long — distinct from failure, which the Reliability Intent owns.

  • none: No escalation; the indicator simply persists.
  • warn: Past a platform-defined threshold, show a message warning the user it is taking longer than usual.
  • async: Past a longer platform-defined duration, recommend taking the task async — a persistent status bar stays present, the user is free to navigate away, and a window prompt warns before they close or leave the app (the task continues in the background). Best when the operation is not a blocking step in a longer flow. The surface that hosts these backgrounded tasks is defined by the Background Task Intent.

Composition with Reliability: timeout policy (a sensible default, configurable per operation class — short for quick calls, long for uploads) and failure handling live in the Reliability Intent. Loader's escalation governs the experience while the operation is still running; Reliability takes over once it times out or fails.

Lifecycle States

The full state machine for a loading operation. Components can react to any state transition.

  • idle: No operation in progress. Default state.
  • pending: Operation started, no result yet. Loading indicator is active. Carries a version/generation token — an immutable snapshot of the input/request state at dispatch time. On resolution, if the current input version has changed, the result is dropped (stale-resolve prevention, cross-cutting req #1).
  • success: Operation completed successfully. Indicator may briefly show confirmation before returning to idle.
  • error: Operation failed. Error state may persist until dismissed or retried.
  • stale: Showing previous data while a refresh is in progress. Combines with transition context.
  • filtering: Re-querying an already-open collection on new filter text (combobox / search-as-you-type). A pending sub-state that keeps prior results visible; debounce the query (see timing) and drop stale resolutions via the version token.
  • loadingMore: Fetching the next page of an infinite / paginated collection. Surfaces as an announced progress row at the list end, never a blocking overlay — composes the Windowed Collection Intent.

Hierarchy Aggregation

Validation and form hierarchies compose the loader lifecycle: a parent's pending state rolls up from its children — pending if any descendant is pending; otherwise success/error/idle per precedence. This aggregation surfaces the full async tree's readiness, enabling submission gates and progress indication. Validation composes this as its async-orchestration meta-intent (cross-cutting req #3: "pending aggregation + submit awaits in-flight async").

Label Conventions

Recommended conventions for loading indicator labels, drawn from design system research (Fluent, Spectrum).

  • Casing: Sentence case ("Loading data..." not "LOADING DATA").
  • Suffix: Trailing ellipsis to convey ongoing activity.
  • Form: Gerund / "-ing" verbs ("Loading", "Saving", "Submitting").
  • Length: Three words or fewer for conciseness.

Interface Protocol

type BlockingStrategy = 'optimistic' | 'soft' | 'hard' | 'replacement';
type LoaderScope = 'element' | 'region' | 'viewport';
type LoaderProgress = 'determinate' | 'indeterminate';
type LoaderContext = 'initial' | 'transition';
type LoaderIndicator = 'loader' | 'skeleton';
type LoaderHoldFloor = 'none' | 'minimum';
type LoaderEscalation = 'none' | 'warn' | 'async';
type LoaderState = 'idle' | 'pending' | 'success' | 'error' | 'stale' | 'filtering' | 'loadingMore';

interface LoaderIntent {
    // How the UI is blocked
    strategy: BlockingStrategy;
    // What part of the UI is affected
    scope: LoaderScope;
    // Whether progress is measurable
    progress: LoaderProgress;
    // Whether prior content exists
    context: LoaderContext;
    // When the loader appears (referencing Config.timings)
    timing: 'immediate' | 'debounced' | string;
    // Indeterminate indicator kind (animation vs content-shaped placeholder)
    indicator?: LoaderIndicator;
    // Hold a shown indicator for a minimum duration to prevent flash
    holdFloor?: LoaderHoldFloor;
    // How an over-long but still-running wait is surfaced
    escalation?: LoaderEscalation;
    // Accessibility label
    label?: string;
    // Motion bindings per lifecycle state
    animations?: Partial<Record<LoaderState, string>>;
}

Provisioning Example

const LoaderConfig = {
    timings: {
        debounced: 400,
        complex: 800
    },
    defaults: {
        region: { strategy: 'soft', timing: 'debounced', context: 'transition' }
    }
};

injector Application {
    provide { Loader } to '@web-intents' from './components/GlobalLoader';
    provide { LoaderConfig } to '@web-intents/loader';
}

Consumer Integration

Blocks that consume the Loader Intent resolve it from the injector chain via customContexts:loaderIntent and select the matching Resource Loader trait for their loading behavior.

// Consumer (e.g., Router) resolves the intent
const intent = InjectorRoot.getProviderOf(element, 'customContexts:loaderIntent');

// Select trait based on declared strategy
switch (intent?.strategy) {
    case 'replacement':
        // Swap content with skeleton/spinner
        return withReplacement(withSkeleton(BaseElement));
    case 'hard':
        // Overlay with backdrop, trap focus
        return withHardBlocking(BaseElement);
    case 'optimistic':
        // No visual change, revert on error
        return withOptimistic(BaseElement);
    default:
        // Soft: aria-busy + pointer-events:none
        return withSoftBlocking(BaseElement);
}

Current consumers: Router (route transitions). The same pattern applies to forms, data grids, and any block with async operations.

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Both support determinate and indeterminate. Naming uses 'progress indicators' (not 'spinner' or 'loader').

components: Inline Loading (four-state: inactive → active → success → error)
designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Four-state Inline Loading is the most complete lifecycle model. Progressive loading pattern: skeleton first, then primary content, then secondary. Never skeleton toasts, modals, or overflow menus.
Size scale (S/M/L/XL). Width constraints (48px min, 768px max for bars). Labels: sentence case with trailing ellipsis. RTL reverses bar fill; circles always spin clockwise.
Labels: short -ing verbs, ≤3 words, trailing ellipsis. Never rewind a progress bar. Switch from indeterminate to determinate once enough data is available.
Never display inaccurate progress. Always allow cancellation for long operations. Activity indicators are non-interactive and must disappear on completion.
No dedicated loading components. Philosophy: progressive enhancement and server rendering avoid loading states by architecture. Relevant for the 'optimistic' strategy baseline.

uxResearch.title: Timing Thresholds
findings: < 1 second: Show nothing — any indicator would flash annoyingly
1–10 seconds: Spinner or skeleton screen (skeleton for known layouts, spinner for unknown)
> 10 seconds: Determinate progress bar — users need to see how much is left; spinners lose credibility

uxResearch.title: Skeleton Screen Types
findings: Animated shimmer: Best — wave/pulse animation conveys activity and outperforms static
Static placeholder: Good — gray blocks matching layout structure
Frame-only: Harmful — showing only chrome (header/footer) without content placeholders makes users assume the page is broken

uxResearch.title: Perceived Performance
findings: Skeleton screens led to ~300ms faster perceived load vs. spinners (Facebook study)
Uncertain waits feel longer than known waits — favor determinate indicators
Unexplained waits feel longer — provide labels and context
Showing page structure (skeletons) reduces anxiety and cognitive load when content appears
People overestimate passive waits by 36% (Maister's psychology of waiting)

uxResearch.title: Don't Hide Visible Content
findings: When content is already visible, keep it visible (dimmed/stale) rather than replacing with a loading fallback
Initial loads use fallbacks; transitions preserve existing content
Replacing visible content with a spinner feels like regression

researchGaps: Skeleton exclusion rules not defined — Carbon research shows the 'replacement' strategy should never apply to toasts, modals, overflow menus, or loaders themselves. Consider an excludeFrom convention.

name: Validation Intent
summary: Interop contract for form validation: observable states and events across control/group/form/nested levels. Defines per-severity commit policies and per-level vocabulary to enable swappable and combinable validation implementations. Composes Loader (async), unified severity vocabulary (Feedback/Message), and Reliability (mechanism failure) intents.

context.description: Where validation messages are displayed / which element commits the state

level.description: Severity + commit policy. Part of unified vocabulary (error/warning/info/success) plus validation-specific caution. error→rejected/blocks-submit, warning/info→committed, caution→held-until-confirm.

execution.description: When validity is computed: on user input (change), blur, or form submission only

visibility.description: When messages are displayed to user: immediate, when field dirty, after touch, or after submission attempt

commitment.description: When invalid values enter form state: immediately on input (full), or buffered locally until blur/submit (deferred)

description:
Meta-Intent Composition

Validation is not standalone — it composes three shared meta-intents:

  • Loader Intent (async lifecycle): Async validators emit pending states. Pending aggregates up the form hierarchy to gate submission.
  • Unified severity vocabulary: level shares the semantic axis (error/warning/info/success + caution) with Feedback (severity) and Message (tone). One severity vocabulary across all messaging ensures consistent tone.
  • Reliability Intent (mechanism failure): Validation owns input invalidity (user must fix the data). Reliability owns mechanism failure (validator crashed, server unreachable). The split prevents conflating input errors with operation failures.

Message Context (Dimension: context)

  • control: Inline message anchored to a single input field.
  • group: Message anchored to a fieldset, composite control, or field array. Error attaches to first errored member by DOM order.
  • form: Aggregate error summary at top of form, ordered by DOM. Links jump to errored fields. Focus moves to summary on submit-blocked.
  • root: Global message (system-wide validation, not field-bound). Never sets aria-invalid on individual fields.

Severity & Commit Policy (Dimension: level)

  • error: Substantive. Blocks form submission. Sets aria-invalid=true. Only error-level can block.
  • warning: Advisory. Submission allowed. No aria-invalid (user-advisory only).
  • caution: Critical advisory. Submission paused until user confirms override (e.g., "Continue anyway?").
  • info: Educational / helper text. Submission allowed. Complements the primary message.
  • [custom]: App-defined levels (e.g., audit). App specifies commit behavior.

Interaction States (Observable at every level)

  • Dirty vs Pristine: Did the value change from baseline?
  • Touched vs Untouched: Has the user focused the control?
  • Submitted: Has the form submission attempt started?
  • Pending: Is an async validator in flight?

Message Display Gating (Dimension: visibility)

When are validation messages shown to the user?

  • immediate: Show messages as soon as validity changes (eager).
  • dirty: Show only after the value has changed from baseline.
  • touched: Show only after the user has focused the control.
  • submitted: Show only after a form submission attempt.

Dynamic policy: Once a field errors, validation may flip to eager (on-change) until fixed, then revert to lazy (on-blur). This "reward early, punish late" pattern prevents annoying re-validation while encouraging rapid correction.

Data Commitment (Dimension: commitment)

When do invalid values enter form state? (UX consequence for autosave, downstream safety, user input preservation)

  • full: Values written to form state on every input, valid or invalid. Autosave gates on validation result. User input always persists (even broken dates). Downstream code must handle invalid states defensively.
  • deferred: Invalid values buffered locally; only committed to form state on blur, submit, or explicit event. Autosave works safely (only valid data reaches it). Trade-off: user loses buffered input on page refresh before commit.

Granular per-field/per-event/per-severity: Commitment is not form-wide. A form can have email deferring on input but committing on blur; errors prevent commit while warnings commit; custom strategies gate commit on severity or field-specific rules. Registry-based (see Validation Block) — full and deferred are built-in; teams register custom strategies (optimistic, staged, severity-gated, reconcile) via CustomCommitmentPolicyRegistry.

Per-Level Contract

Control (Single Field)

  • Observable: Is it valid? Is it pending? Is it dirty/touched? What message (if any) should show?
  • Events: User changed value, user blurred, validation started/ended, became valid/invalid, message appeared/disappeared.
  • A11y: aria-invalid (error only), aria-describedby (message anchor), :user-invalid/:user-valid (visibility gate).

Group (Fieldset or Composite Control)

  • Observable: Is the group valid (all members valid)? If invalid, which member errored first?
  • Dependency: If one field depends on another (requiredIf), changing the dependency revalidates dependents.
  • Completion: How many members are complete vs. empty (useful for multi-step forms).

Form (Full Document)

  • Submission lifecycle: idle → validating (await pending) → blocked (show errors) OR submitting (valid) → submitted/failed.
  • Error summary: DOM-ordered list of all errors. Links anchor to errored controls. Focus moves to summary on submit-blocked (GOV.UK pattern).
  • Rollup: Is the form submittable (not invalid AND not pending)?
  • Server errors: Errors from the server (authentication fail, business rule violation) are injected by field path. They do not wipe on user edit.

Nested Form (Multi-Step, Field Arrays, Wizard)

  • Bubbling: Does child invalidity block parent submission? (participating) Or do children validate in isolation? (isolated)
  • Field arrays: Errors track rows by stable key (not index). Reorder preserves error association.
  • Wizard steps: Current step must be valid to advance. Previous steps remain valid (back-nav preserves values and baseline).

Optional Features (Capability Bundles)

Not all validation systems support all features. Apps declare which features they use:

  • async: Async validators (server-side checks, network calls).
  • cross-field: One field's validity depends on another field's value (e.g., password confirmation).
  • conditional: Fields appear/disappear based on values. Validation rules change dynamically.
  • field-array: Repeating sections (e.g., multiple addresses). Add/remove/reorder rows.
  • wizard: Multi-step form with per-step validation gates.
  • error-summary: Form-level error summary with focus-on-submit-blocked (GOV.UK pattern).
  • severity: Warning/caution/info beyond binary error/valid.
  • schema: Vendor-neutral schema input (Standard Schema, Zod, Valibot, etc.).
  • server-reconciliation: Server errors merged with client errors by field path.

Web Standards Alignment

Standard How We Use It
Constraint Validation API Native validity gating and aria-invalid anchoring via ElementInternals.setValidity().
ARIA Form Validation aria-invalid (error level only), aria-describedby/aria-errormessage (message anchoring), aria-live (error summary updates), role=alert (error summary role).
:user-invalid / :user-valid Native display gate. Styles apply only after user touch/submit. Maps to visibility dimension. Baseline Oct 2023.

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Dual model: submit-only timing (discourages live/blur), error summary at top with focus-to-summary + anchor links, inline messages with verbatim parity. Gold standard for placement + a11y.
Comprehensive enum: validateStatus ∈ {success, warning, error, validating}. validateTrigger per-field override (change/blur/submit). validateFirst (short-circuit).
On-blur default + helper-text→error-message swap. Progress-indicator step error state. Four-state Inline Loading (inactive→active→success→error).
Error replaces helper text. Warm error hue. Native setValidity() support via custom elements.
Native binary validity (only error level). setValidity() carries an anchor element for focus. :user-invalid gates display to touched|submitted. Beyond-native: pending, severity, cross-field.

uxResearch.title: Reward early, punish late
findings: Once a field errors, switch from lazy to eager validation (on change instead of blur).
On fix, clear the error message immediately.
Revert to lazy (on blur) once the field is clean again.
This dynamic flip prevents annoying re-validation while encouraging rapid correction.

uxResearch.title: Live validation harm
findings: Live/blur validation can raise error rates and user frustration.
GOV.UK actively discourages it; ship submit-only as the safe default.
If a design requires live, it must be opt-in and carefully tested for UX impact.

uxResearch.title: Async generation/version token requirement
uxResearch.source: React Hook Form, Final Form, Vuelidate issue trackers
findings: Every "stale resolve clobbers newer error" bug stems from missing generation tokens.
Results from async validators dispatched for old input versions must be dropped.
RHF #1688, Final Form #420, Vuelidate #364 all trace to this.

uxResearch.title: Validity merge order (deterministic precedence)
findings: Concurrent sources (sync, async, manual, schema) must have a declared merge order.
Without it: "server error wiped on blur", "schema overwrites manual error", "root toggles isValid" bugs.
Formik #706, RHF #9982 trace to undefined precedence.

researchGaps: Reward-early/punish-late is a dynamic flip (not a flat execution value). Interaction state gates display, not compute timing.
Native validity is strictly binary (error only). Warning/caution/info are beyond-native, advisory-only, never block submission.
visibility:dirty has no native pseudo-class. Must be tracked explicitly in interaction state.
Async needs version token + aggregation + submit-await. Native CVAPI has none.
Concurrent source merge order must be normalized (cross-cutting requirement across all libraries).
Root/global error must block submit without corrupting per-field validity (the RHF "isValid" bug family).
Capability/feature modularity + adherence detection is a separate meta-layer (see spec-versioning brief).
DI concern interfaces and their hand-off contracts are normative only at Level 2; Level 1 needs only observable state/events.

name: Modal Intent
summary: Protocol defining dialog behavior including stacking, scrolling, sizing, and dismissal strategies.

placement.description: Visual position of the modal surface

mode.description: Whether the modal blocks background interaction

dismissal.description: How the modal can be closed

description:
Placement & Docking

  • center: Standard dialog.
  • top/bottom: Sheet/Drawer behavior (Mobile standard).
  • start/end: Sidebar/Panel behavior.
  • fill: Full-screen override.

Focus Management

A modal surface composes the Focus Containment Intent for focus behavior — trapping focus inside, marking the background inert, setting initial focus, and restoring focus to the trigger on close — rather than redefining it here. A mode: modal surface defaults to trap + inert; mode: modeless defaults to none + interactive.

Lifecycle Events

  • enter: Modal opens and becomes active.
  • exit: Modal closes.
  • recede: Modal moves to background (when a new stacked modal opens).
  • advance: Modal moves to foreground (when the top stacked modal closes).

Design System Alignment

SystemComponentPlacementBackdropDismissal
Material 3Basic Dialogcenterdimmedlight
Material 3Full-screenfilltransparentexplicit
CarbonModaltopdimmedexplicit
Apple HIGSheetbottomdimmedlight
Radix UIDialogcenterdimmedlight

Interface Protocol

type Dismissal = 'light' | 'explicit' | 'none';
type Placement = 'center' | 'top' | 'bottom' | 'start' | 'end' | 'fill';
type Backdrop = 'dimmed' | 'transparent' | 'blur';

interface ModalIntent {
    // Role (ARIA)
    role: 'dialog' | 'alertdialog';
    // Visual placement
    placement: Placement;
    // Interaction Mode (Blocks background?)
    mode: 'modal' | 'modeless';
    // Mobile Snap Points (Sheet)
    detents?: { initial: 'medium' | 'large' | 'full'; stops: string[] };
    // Backdrop visual
    backdrop: Backdrop;
    // How the modal can be closed
    dismissal: Dismissal | 'gesture';
    // Focus Management — see the Focus Containment Intent
    focus: FocusContainmentIntent;
    // Allow other modals on top?
    stackable: boolean;
    // Scroll strategy
    scrolling: 'contained' | 'viewport';
    // Responsive sizing map
    size: 'auto' | 'fullscreen' | Record<string, string>;
    // Motion bindings
    animations?: Partial>;
}

name: Action Intent
summary: Protocol defining the semantic hierarchy (levels) of interactive elements to establish visual priority.

level.description: The semantic weight determining visual prominence

busy.description: What the user experiences when the action is pending

groupOrdering.description: How actions are ordered in a group

groupSizing.description: How actions are sized in a group

description:
Action Levels

The semantic weight of an action, determining its visual prominence. Every design system converges on this hierarchy.

  • primary: Principal call to action. Highest visual weight (Material FAB, Fluent Solid).
  • secondary: Alternative actions. Moderate visual weight (Outlined, Tonal).
  • tertiary: Supplemental actions. Minimal visual weight (Text, Ghost).
  • destructive: Negative actions. Implies danger semantics (typically red).

Grouping & Alignment

Spatial relationships for collections of actions (Dialog footers, Toolbars, Form actions).

  • ordering: Platform-aware (macOS: Primary Last, Windows: Primary First) vs DOM order (as authored).
  • sizing: Stretch (full-width, common on mobile) vs Auto (content-sized, common on desktop).
  • placement: End (Dialogs), Start (Forms), Split (primary and secondary at opposite ends).

Busy State

What the user experiences when an action is pending. The implementing block handles the CSS and layout details.

  • interaction: Whether the action is disabled or remains interactive during the pending state.
  • indicator: Whether a loading indicator appears, and its position relative to the label (start, end, center, overlay).
  • label: Whether the label changes (e.g., "Save" → "Saving..."), hides, or remains as-is.

Interface Protocol

type ActionLevel = 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'tertiary' | 'destructive' | string;
type IndicatorPosition = 'start' | 'end' | 'center' | 'overlay';

interface ActionBusy {
    // Is the action disabled while busy?
    interaction: 'disabled' | 'interactive';
    // Where to show a loading indicator (if any)
    indicator?: IndicatorPosition;
    // Temporary label during pending state
    label?: string;
}

interface ActionGroupIntent {
    alignment: 'start' | 'end' | 'center' | 'justify' | 'split';
    ordering: 'dom' | 'reverse' | 'platform';
    sizing: 'auto' | 'stretch' | 'uniform';
}

interface ActionIntent {
    // The semantic weight
    level: ActionLevel;
    // What the user experiences when the action is pending
    busy?: ActionBusy;
    // Icon configuration (references Icon Intent)
    icon?: { position?: 'start' | 'end' };
    // Grouping Context (for Collections)
    group?: ActionGroupIntent;
}

name: Icon Intent
summary: Protocol defining iconography standards including sizing scales, weight, and positioning strategies.

size.description: The visual size of the icon

description:
Sizing Scale

  • sm: Small (e.g., 16px). For inline text or tight density.
  • md: Medium (e.g., 20px). Standard usage.
  • lg: Large (e.g., 24px). Feature icons or comfortable buttons.
  • xl: Extra Large (e.g., 32px+). Hero or empty states.

Interface Protocol

type IconSize = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg' | 'xl' | string;

interface IconIntent {
    // The visual size identifier
    size: IconSize;
    // Name of the icon asset/glyph
    name: string;
    // Stroke weight (if variable)
    weight?: 'light' | 'regular' | 'bold';
    // Render style
    style?: 'outlined' | 'filled';
}

name: Breakpoint Intent
summary: Protocol defining display adaptation strategies, abstracting viewport tiers and container queries.

strategy.description: How layout rules accumulate as the viewport changes

steps.description: Named tiers describing the user's display context

description:
Adaptation Strategy

How layout rules accumulate or subtract as the viewport changes. This determines the mental model for authoring responsive layouts.

  • mobile-first: Start small, add complexity as space grows. Standard approach (Tailwind, Bootstrap).
  • desktop-first: Start large, remove features as space shrinks. Legacy approach.
  • strict-range: Each size tier is an isolated bucket — no accumulation. Used by Material Design 3.

Semantic Steps

Named tiers that describe the user's display context, independent of specific pixel values. The implementing block maps these to concrete media/container queries.

  • compact: Phones in portrait. Single-column layouts, stacked navigation.
  • medium: Tablets or phones in landscape. Two-column layouts, rail navigation.
  • expanded: Desktops or large tablets. Multi-column layouts, persistent navigation.
  • [custom]: Application-defined tiers (e.g., ultrawide for multi-pane dashboards).

Logical Groups

Aliases that combine steps for convenience. Allows components to query "am I in a small context?" without knowing specific tier names.

  • small: Groups compact (equivalent to "mobile").
  • large: Groups expanded and above (equivalent to "desktop").

Interface Protocol

type AdaptationStrategy = 'mobile-first' | 'desktop-first' | 'strict-range';

interface BreakpointIntent {
    // How layout rules accumulate
    strategy: AdaptationStrategy;
    // Ordered list of semantic step names (smallest to largest)
    steps: string[];
    // Logical groups that alias one or more steps
    aliases?: Record<string, string[]>;
}

name: Typography Intent
summary: Protocol abstracting visual hierarchy from semantic HTML, normalizing roles across Design Systems.

role.description: The semantic purpose of the text

truncation.description: How text that overflows its box is cut, and how the full value is recovered

description:
Role Definition

  • display: Expressive/Decorative (Hero, Splash). Low density.
  • heading: Structural hierarchy (H1-H6 markers).
  • body: focused on Readability (Long-form content).
  • label: focused on Legibility (UI Components, Buttons).
  • code: Monospace technical data.

Truncation

How overflowing text is cut. Always truncate visually (CSS) while keeping the full string as the element's text content — never hard-slice the string mid-grapheme, and keep at least ~4 meaningful characters.

  • clip: Hard cut at the box edge, no indicator. Use only where the cut is obvious from context.
  • ellipsis: text-overflow: ellipsis — the conventional single-line cut with a trailing ….
  • fade: A gradient mask fades the trailing edge; avoids the visual weight of an ellipsis.

Recovery & accessibility: whenever text is truncated, provide a way to read the full value — a title / tooltip for short labels, an expand affordance for longer ones. Front-load the distinguishing words so they survive the cut. Because the full text remains the accessible name, an interactive truncated label still satisfies WCAG 2.5.3 Label in Name — do not replace it with an unrelated aria-label. This axis is the home for the Accessible truncation paradigm reused by table cells, tabs, breadcrumbs, chips, and dropdown option labels.

Interface Protocol

type TypographyRole = 'display' | 'heading' | 'body' | 'label' | 'code';
// T-shirt scale relative to the Role base size
type TypographyScale = 'xs' | 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg' | 'xl';

interface ToneFeatures {
    // Force monospace numbers (Data Grids)
    tabular?: boolean;
    // Overflow behavior
    truncation?: 'clip' | 'ellipsis' | 'fade';
    // Disable definition-based optical sizing
    opticalSizing?: 'auto' | 'none';
}

interface TypographyIntent {
    // The semantic purpose
    role: TypographyRole;
    // Relative size/emphasis within that role
    scale?: TypographyScale;
    // Rendering constraints
    features?: ToneFeatures;
}

name: Surface Intent
summary: Protocol defining Z-Axis depth, material texture (Glass/Solid), and interactions across lighting modes.
description:
Elevation Semantics (Z-Axis)

  • 0: Base. Application background.
  • 1-2: Grouping content. (Cards, Lists).
  • 3-5: Floating content. (Sticky Headers, Modals, Tooltips).

Material Texture

  • solid: Opaque. Standard for Material Design 3 (Surface Tones).
  • glass: Blurred background. Standard for macOS/iOS (Vibrancy) and Windows (Mica/Acrylic).
  • transparent: Layout-only containment.

Interface Protocol

type SurfaceMaterial = 'solid' | 'glass' | 'transparent';
type SurfaceInteraction = 'static' | 'lift' | 'scale';

interface SurfaceIntent {
    // Z-axis position relative to parent (0-5)
    elevation: number;
    
    // Physical texture of the surface
    texture: SurfaceMaterial;
    
    // Visual variation (Default vs High Contrast)
    variant: 'default' | 'alt';
    
    // Interaction physics
    interaction: SurfaceInteraction;
}

name: Feedback Intent
summary: Protocol defining transient, non-blocking messaging (Toasts/Snackbars) and their queuing logic. Severity dimension participates in the unified severity/tone vocabulary shared with Message and Validation intents.

severity.description: Semantic weight of the message. Part of unified severity vocabulary: info (informational), success (confirmation), warning (advisory), error (failure).

description:
Placement Logic

  • top-end: Standard for Desktop notifications (Toasts).
  • bottom-start: Standard for Material Design (Snackbars). Avoids FAB interaction.
  • top-center: Mobile alerts.

Severity Semantics

severity is part of a unified severity/tone vocabulary shared across Feedback (transient), Message (structural), and Validation (input states). All three use the same semantic set:

  • info: Informational. Neutral tone. No action required.
  • success: Confirmation. Positive outcome achieved.
  • warning: Advisory. Issue detected but not blocking. User may act.
  • error: Failure. Problem occurred. User action required.

Timing & Duration

  • short: ~4000ms. For simple confirmations ("Saved").
  • long: ~10000ms. If text > 60 chars.
  • indefinite: Remains until dismissed. Required if an Action is present (Accessibility).

Interface Protocol

type FeedbackPlacement = 'top-start' | 'top-end' | 'bottom-start' | 'bottom-end' | 'top-center' | 'bottom-center';
type FeedbackSeverity = 'info' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'error';

interface FeedbackAction {
    label: string;
    intent: string;
}

interface FeedbackIntent {
    // Positioning strategy
    placement: FeedbackPlacement;
    // Semantic weight (shared severity vocabulary)
    severity: FeedbackSeverity;
    // Persistence
    duration: 'short' | 'long' | 'indefinite';
    // Queue Management (1 = Single/Replace, >1 = Stack)
    stackLimit: number;
    // Optional interactive element (e.g. "Undo")
    action?: FeedbackAction;
}

name: Selection Intent
summary: Protocol defining choice behaviors (Single vs Multi) and state urgency, distinguishing interactions from form data.

model.description: How many items can be selected

immediacy.description: When the selection state is committed

grouping.description: Whether options are flat or organized into labeled groups

description:
Choice Models

  • single: Mutually exclusive. Renders as Radio (Forms), Switch (Settings), or Select (Compact).
  • multiple: Accumulative. Renders as Checkbox (List) or Filter Chips (Toolbar).

Immediacy Profile

  • live: State changes immediately (Settings toggle).
  • buffered: State changes on submission (Form field).

Grouping

Options may be flat or organized into labeled grouped sets — the Grouping paradigm, reused by menus, command palettes, and faceted lists.

  • Native: <optgroup label="…"> — the label is required and is what assistive tech announces as group membership; groups do not nest.
  • Custom (ARIA): wrap each group's options in role="group" with aria-labelledby pointing at a visible heading; group headings are not focusable options.

Interface Protocol

type SelectionModel = 'single' | 'multiple';
type SelectionVariant = 'boolean' | 'item' | 'range';

interface SelectionIntent {
    // How many can be selected
    model: SelectionModel;
    // When the state is committed
    immediacy: 'live' | 'buffered';
    // Visual metaphor
    variant: SelectionVariant;
    // Constraints
    constraints?: { min?: number; max?: number };
}

name: Input Intent
summary: Protocol for text entry standardization including affordances, privacy masking, and visual variants.

affordances.description: Interaction helpers shown with the input

description:
Visual Variants

  • outlined: High contrast (Material default). Defined boundary.
  • filled: High emphasis background (Fluent/Material).
  • borderless: Minimalist (iOS List style).

Affordances

  • clear: 'X' button to reset the value — the Clearable value paradigm. Shown only when a value is present; carries an explicit accessible name ("Clear"); is keyboard-reachable; and returns focus to the input after clearing. Keep it decoupled from Esc — in a combobox Esc closes the popup, while clearing is a separate, opt-in action — so users don't lose data accidentally. Reused by search fields, comboboxes, tag inputs, and filters.
  • reveal: Eye icon for passwords.
  • copy: Clipboard action.

Interface Protocol

type InputVariant = 'outlined' | 'filled' | 'borderless';
type InputAffordance = 'clear' | 'reveal' | 'copy';

interface InputIntent {
    // Visual style
    variant: InputVariant;
    // Privacy masking (dots)
    masking: boolean;
    // Interaction helpers
    affordances: InputAffordance[];
    // State
    status: 'enabled' | 'disabled' | 'readonly';
}

name: Navigation Intent
summary: UX vocabulary for in-app navigation: information-architecture structure (hierarchical/lateral/linear) plus the orthogonal behaviors a view change carries — history contribution, scroll landing, transition direction, leave-guarding, and URL persistence. Composes prefetch (eagerness), motion (animation feel), and focus-delegation (focus reset).

structure.description: The information-architecture model: how views are organized and how users traverse between them

history.description: What this navigation contributes to the session history — i.e. what the browser Back button does afterward

scroll.description: Where the viewport lands after the view swaps

transition.description: The directionality of the view transition (forward vs back). Animation feel — easing, reduced-motion — defers to the Motion intent

guard.description: Whether leaving the current view can be interrupted (e.g. unsaved changes)

persistence.description: Whether this navigation state is reflected in a deep-linkable, shareable URL or held ephemerally. Grounded in the WHATWG split between the URL (path/search) and the History API state object: url = the URL (survives refresh + share), session = History state (survives traversal but not a fresh share), memory = neither (in-memory only). Unlike history/scroll this rests on that convention rather than a single platform enum; the three-way set is kept because session is a distinct user-perceivable outcome (a wizard step that survives Back/Forward but isn't shareable), subject to review if no block exercises session within two implementing blocks (#057).

description:
The Navigation Intent is the UX vocabulary for moving between views. structure declares the information-architecture shape; the remaining dimensions declare the orthogonal behaviors a single view change carries. Any structure can pair with any behavior — a lateral tab set may still want a deep-linkable URL, a linear wizard may guard against leaving mid-step.

Structure Model

  • hierarchical: Parent/child stack — manages history and back-navigation (Router). Active item marked aria-current="page".
  • lateral: Peer switching (Tabs, Bottom Nav). Active item marked aria-current="page".
  • linear: Sequential steps (Wizard). Locked progression; current step marked aria-current="step".

The correct aria-current token is derived from structure (page for hierarchical/lateral, step for linear) — it is part of the structure contract, not a separate choice.

History Contribution

  • push: New history entry — Back returns to the prior view.
  • replace: Overwrite the current entry — Back skips this view (typical for wizard steps).
  • none: Transient change that never enters the back stack.

Scroll Landing

  • auto: Platform default — restore on back/forward, honor a URL fragment, else top.
  • top: Always reset to the top on a forward navigation.
  • preserve: Keep the current scroll position (correct for in-place tab/filter swaps).
  • manual: The view drives scroll itself.

Transition Direction

  • directional: Forward navigations animate forward, Back animates back (derived from history-traversal direction — browser-initiated Back carries no transition type).
  • fade: Non-directional crossfade.
  • none: No transition.

Direction is owned here; the feel of the animation (natural/immediate/reduced, honoring prefers-reduced-motion) is owned by the Motion intent.

Leave Guard

  • free: Navigation always proceeds.
  • confirm: Intercept and prompt ("unsaved changes — leave?"); the user may proceed. The prompt UI composes with the Modal intent.
  • blocked: Navigation is prevented until a condition clears.

URL Persistence

  • url: State lives in the path/search — bookmarkable, shareable, survives refresh.
  • session: State rides session history — survives traversal but not a fresh share.
  • memory: In-memory only — lost on refresh or share.

Composition

  • Prefetch: speculative loading of the next view is the Prefetch intent's eagerness axis — Navigation composes it, does not own it.
  • Focus reset: where focus goes after a view swap is a referenced contract (WAI-ARIA APG: move to the new view's main heading; platform focusReset) — the closed value set lives with Focus Delegation, not duplicated here.
  • Loader: pending-state UX during a navigation defers to the Loader intent.

name: Anchor Intent
summary: Protocol for tethered surfaces (Tooltips, Popovers, Menus) defining placement, collision handling, and tracking. Position computation is delegated to a shared positioning provider; top-layer escape composes with Web Portals.

type.description: The role of the anchored surface

placement.description: Preferred position of the anchored element relative to its anchor (12-way grid + auto)

collision.description: Ordered fallback pipeline applied when the preferred placement overflows the viewport or a clipping ancestor

strategy.description: How the anchored element stacks. inline stays in normal flow; escape lifts it to the top layer (composes Web Portals)

tracking.description: Whether the position is recomputed as layout changes

dismissal.description: How the surface is closed and where focus returns

description:
Tether Contract

  • tooltip: Informational. Hover/Focus trigger. Non-interactive.
  • popover: Interactive content. Click trigger. Traps focus if modal.
  • menu: Action list. Click/Context trigger. Keyboard navigable.

Placement

The preferred side and alignment of the anchored element relative to its anchor element, using the platform 12-way grid (side × start/center/end). auto defers the choice to the provider (most-available-space).

Collision

An ordered fallback pipeline applied when the preferred placement would overflow.

  • flip: Move to the opposite side (CSS position-try / Floating UI flip).
  • shift: Slide along the axis to stay in view (shift).
  • hide: Hide the anchored element when the anchor is clipped or scrolled off-screen (CSS position-visibility).
  • none: No fallback; honor the requested placement verbatim.

Strategy

  • inline: The anchored element stays in normal flow / stacking context (CSS anchor(), absolute).
  • escape: The anchored element lifts to the top layer to escape clipping ancestors — composed via Web Portals (DOM/context escape) and the Surface Intent (elevation), not re-implemented here.

Dismissal & Focus Return

How the surface closes, and the invariant that focus returns to the trigger on close so keyboard users keep their place.

  • light: Esc and outside-click both dismiss (Popover API light-dismiss). For an interactive surface, the first Esc closes the surface before any inner clear/reset semantics fire.
  • explicit: Closes only via an in-surface control or re-trigger; outside-click does not dismiss. For surfaces whose content must not vanish on an accidental click.
  • modal: Focus is trapped inside the surface; Esc dismisses and focus returns to the trigger. Pairs with a backdrop. The trap + inert + focus-return mechanics are not redefined here — a modal-mode surface composes the Focus Containment Intent, which owns that contract.

The in-widget arrow-key model for a menu surface is owned by the Focus Delegation Intent; this axis governs only how the surface itself is dismissed.

Delegation

This intent declares the what; the how (position math, collision, tracking) is delegated to a registered positioning provider resolved through CustomPositioningRegistry — native-first (CSS Anchor Positioning) by default, or a library adapter (Floating UI). See Web Positioning.

Interface Protocol

type SurfaceRole = 'tooltip' | 'popover' | 'menu';
type Placement = 'top' | 'top-start' | 'top-end'
  | 'right' | 'right-start' | 'right-end'
  | 'bottom' | 'bottom-start' | 'bottom-end'
  | 'left' | 'left-start' | 'left-end' | 'auto';
type CollisionStrategy = 'flip' | 'shift' | 'hide' | 'none';
type AnchorStrategy = 'inline' | 'escape';   // 'escape' composes Web Portals (top layer)

interface AnchorIntent {
    // The role of the anchored surface
    role: SurfaceRole;
    // Preferred position relative to the anchor
    placement: Placement;
    // Ordered fallback pipeline on overflow
    collision: CollisionStrategy[];
    // Normal flow vs. top-layer escape
    strategy: AnchorStrategy;
    // Gap from the anchor edge (px)
    offset?: number;
    // Recompute on scroll / resize
    tracking?: 'static' | 'follow';
    // Render a pointer toward the anchor
    arrow?: boolean;
}

Implementation note — native flip needs position: fixed

The native CSS Anchor Positioning strategy positions the surface position: fixed, not absolute. position-try-fallbacks evaluates overflow against the surface’s containing block, so an absolute surface (containing block = a positioned ancestor or the document-sized initial containing block) sees room below and never flips near a viewport edge. fixed makes the containing block the viewport, so flip-block/flip-inline engage at the real edge — and anchor positioning still tracks the trigger on scroll, no script. (Backlog #161.)

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Native, Baseline 2026. Unifies placement + collision fallback in CSS while leaving layering (top layer) to the Popover API. We adopt this separation: the Anchor Intent owns placement, Web Portals owns escape.

Handles top-layer stacking and light dismiss, but NOT positioning — it delegates placement to CSS Anchor Positioning via the implicit anchor. Confirms positioning and layering are orthogonal concerns.

Combines anchoring + collision + escape in one JS engine. Our dimension and option vocabulary mirrors it 1:1 so the adapter maps directly; Tier-3 middleware (autoPlacement, size, inline) stay adapter-only.
Headless side/align maps to our placement; avoidCollisions/collisionPadding map to the collision pipeline. Validates exposing collision as a first-class, configurable axis.
Menus reposition to remain on-screen and align to the anchor edge; rich tooltips persist and are interactive. Informs the tooltip/popover/menu role split.

researchGaps: Escape delegation depth — should strategy:'escape' drive the native Popover top layer directly, or always route through Web Portals for context preservation? Define the handoff. (Note: the dismissal axis now owns Esc/outside-click close + focus-return; this gap is specifically about top-layer escape.)
Arrow placement math — ship a basic built-in arrow in the native provider, or always defer to an adapter (Floating UI arrow())?
RTL and safe-area handling (notch, virtual keyboard) in the native-first default provider.

name: Temporal Intent
summary: Protocol abstracting date/time selection paradigms across mouse, touch, and keyboard modalities.

presentation.description: The visual paradigm for date/time selection

description:
Presentation Paradigms

  • media: Spatial surfaces. Wall calendars (Grid) or Analog Clocks.
  • linear: Sequential lists. Scrolling Wheels (iOS) or Listboxes.
  • input: Text-based. Masked inputs or simple native fallbacks.

Granularity

  • point: Single instance (Date/Time).
  • range: Span between two points.
  • multi: Disconnected sets of points.

name: Layout Intent
summary: Protocol defining the application shell structure and region mechanics (Drawers, Rails, Panels).

behavior.description: How regions interact with the main content area

description:
Region Behavior

  • push: Region consumes space, shifting the main content (Desktop).
  • overlay: Region floats above content with a backdrop (Mobile).
  • rail: Region collapses to a thin strip (Icons) rather than disappearing.

Anatomy

  • shell: The global viewport container.
  • pane: A resizable subdivision of the shell.
  • dock: A sticky region (Header/Footer).

name: Message Intent
summary: Protocol defining communication priority (Attention) and structural anatomy across alerts, banners, and dialogs. Tone dimension participates in the unified severity/tone vocabulary shared with Feedback and Validation intents.

attention.description: Priority level determining how the message interrupts the user

tone.description: Semantic meaning conveyed by the message. Part of unified severity vocabulary: neutral↔info, positive↔success, caution↔warning, negative↔error.

description:
Attention Hierarchy

  • ambient: Low priority. Status bars or helper text.
  • routine: Standard priority. Toasts or inline notes.
  • urgent: High priority. Banners or System Alerts.
  • critical: Blocking priority. Dialogs or Screen Takeovers.

Tone Semantics

tone is part of a unified severity/tone vocabulary shared across Message (structural), Feedback (transient), and Validation (input states). The semantic mapping aligns all three:

  • neutral / info: Informational. No action required.
  • positive / success: Confirmation. Positive outcome achieved.
  • caution / warning: Advisory. Issue detected but not blocking. User may act.
  • negative / error: Failure. Problem occurred. User action required.

name: Reliability Intent
summary: Protocol defining error recovery strategies, retry policies, and outcome handling for mechanism failures (system/operation errors). Distinct from input-invalidity (validation domain): Reliability handles operation failures (timeout, crash, network); Validation handles input errors (field wrong, value out of range).

tolerance.description: How severely the user is affected by failure

recovery.description: What the user experiences during system recovery

description:
Scope: Mechanism Failure (not Input Invalidity)

Reliability is strictly for operation/system failure, not validation errors:

  • Input-invalidity (Validation domain): "The user's email is malformed" or "These passwords don't match". This is an expected outcome; the field is wrong, and the user must fix it. Validation owns this.
  • Mechanism failure (Reliability domain): "The uniqueness check timed out" or "The server is unreachable" or "The async validator crashed". This is an unexpected system problem. Reliability owns this.

Boundary with Loader: a wait that is merely taking too long but still running is a Loader Intent concern (its escalation dimension: warn, then go-async). Reliability takes over only once that wait times out or fails — it owns the timeout default (configurable per operation class) and what the user experiences during recovery.

A validation scenario that includes async (e.g., checking if an email is already registered) may emit a Reliability error if the check itself fails (server down), but a Validation error if the email is taken. The distinction prevents conflating the two error families.

Failure Tolerance

How severely the user is affected when an operation fails. This determines the visual response and the user's ability to continue.

  • forgivable: Operation failed but the user can continue. Communicated silently (logged) or with minimal disruption (toast). App state remains valid.
  • degraded: Partial failure. The UI renders with missing data or fallback content. The user can still interact but the experience is incomplete.
  • terminal: Broken state. The component cannot render meaningfully. Requires replacement with an error boundary or a full-page error state.

Recovery Experience

What the user experiences while the system attempts to recover from a failure. The implementing block decides the technical retry strategy (timing, backoff algorithm).

  • automatic: The system retries silently. The user sees a loading state but takes no action.
  • manual: The user is presented with a "Retry" affordance and decides when to try again.
  • none: No recovery is attempted. The failure state is final.

Fallback Content

What the user sees in place of the failed content.

  • skeleton: Structural placeholder, implying the content may load later.
  • empty: Explicit empty state with messaging (e.g., "No data available").
  • previous: Stale data from the last successful load, marked as outdated.

Interface Protocol

type FailureTolerance = 'forgivable' | 'degraded' | 'terminal';
type RecoveryExperience = 'automatic' | 'manual' | 'none';
type FallbackContent = 'skeleton' | 'empty' | 'previous';

interface ReliabilityIntent {
    // How severely the user is affected
    tolerance: FailureTolerance;
    // What the user experiences during recovery
    recovery: RecoveryExperience;
    // What replaces the failed content
    fallback?: FallbackContent;
}

name: Type-Ahead Intent
summary: Protocol for keyboard type-ahead navigation: typed characters move focus to the matching item, with a buffer that resets after an idle timeout. Distinct from filtering.

matching.description: How typed characters map to items

reset.description: When the accumulated character buffer clears

wrap.description: Focus behavior at the ends of the collection

description:
Matching Strategy

How typed characters are mapped to an item. Type-ahead moves focus to an existing item — it never removes or hides items (that is filtering, see aria-autocomplete="list" on a combobox).

  • prefix: Characters accumulate into a buffer; focus moves to the first item whose label starts with the buffer.
  • cycle: A repeated single character cycles focus through items whose label starts with that character.
  • prefix-then-cycle: Recommended default. Matches WAI-ARIA APG and native <select> — characters typed in rapid succession accumulate into a prefix, while a lone repeated key cycles among same-initial items.

Reset Timing

When the character buffer is discarded so the next keystroke starts a fresh search.

  • debounced: Buffer clears after an idle timeout (defaults to 500ms, the APG / native <select> convention).
  • custom: Application-defined timeout via resetMs.

Wrap Behavior

What happens when matching passes the last item.

  • wrap: Search wraps around to the start of the collection (default).
  • clamp: Search stops at the ends without wrapping.

Label Source
